nodes.label: Normative Ethics
nodes.wd_description: study of ethical action; branch of philosophical ethics that investigates questions about how one morally ought to act and examines standards for the rightness and wrongness of actions

nodes.label: Metaethics
nodes.wd_description: branch of ethics seeking to understand ethical properties

nodes.label: Consequentialism
nodes.wd_description: class of ethical theory basing standards of right & wrong on the consequences of actions

nodes.label: Utilitarianism
nodes.wd_description: ethical theory which holds that the right action is the one that maximizes aggregate well-being

nodes.label: Rule Utilitarianism
nodes.wd_description: form of utilitarianism that says an action is right as it conforms to a rule that leads to the greatest good

nodes.label: Categorical Imperative
nodes.wd_description: central concept in Kantian moral philosophy
nodes.note: Q221373 (used in early plan docs) is actually deontology — corrected 2026-06-11

nodes.label: Hypothetical Imperative
nodes.wd_description: Kantian philosophical concept; antonym of categorical imperative

nodes.label: Maxim
nodes.note: Kantian sense: subjective principle of action; NOT aphorism/saying — no Wikidata item for this sense (reviewed 2026-06-11)

nodes.label: Good Will
nodes.note: Kantian sense; NOT goodwill (accounting) or benevolence generally — no Wikidata item for this sense (reviewed 2026-06-11)

nodes.label: Duty
nodes.wd_description: legal or moral requirement to take a certain course of action

nodes.label: Kingdom of Ends
nodes.wd_description: Kant’s thought experiment about a world in which all humans are treated as ends, not as means

nodes.label: Autonomy
nodes.wd_description: concept found in moral, political, and bioethical philosophy
nodes.note: moral-philosophical sense; NOT political autonomy of regions

nodes.label: Moral Anti-Realism
nodes.note: no Wikidata item (reviewed 2026-06-11)

nodes.label: Moral Relativism
nodes.wd_description: philosophical positions about the differences in moral judgments across peoples and cultures

nodes.label: Moral Nihilism
nodes.wd_description: meta-ethical view that nothing is intrinsically moral or immoral

nodes.label: Moral Cognitivism
nodes.wd_description: the meta-ethical view that ethical sentences express propositions and can therefore be true or false
nodes.note: metaethical thesis; NOT cognitivism in psychology

nodes.label: Non-Cognitivism
nodes.wd_description: the meta-ethical view that ethical sentences do not express propositions and thus cannot be true or false

nodes.label: Emotivism
nodes.wd_description: metaethical view that moral statements are expressions of emotional attitudes rather than objective truths

nodes.label: Expressivism
nodes.wd_description: metaethical theory that moral sentences are not descriptive, but express an evaluative attitude toward an object

nodes.label: Prescriptivism
nodes.wd_description: metaethical view that ethical sentences function similarly to imperatives which are universalizable—whoever makes a moral judgment is committed to the same judgment in any situation where the same relevant facts obtain
nodes.note: Hare's metaethics; NOT linguistic prescriptivism

nodes.label: Moral Intuitionism
nodes.wd_description: family of views in moral epistemology
nodes.note: NOT mathematical intuitionism

nodes.label: Contractarianism
nodes.note: Hobbesian/Gauthier tradition, distinct from Scanlonian contractualism; Wikidata only has the latter (reviewed 2026-06-11)

nodes.label: Ethical Egoism
nodes.wd_description: ethical position that moral agents should act in their own self-interest

nodes.label: Altruism
nodes.wd_description: principle or practice of concern for the welfare of others
nodes.note: ethical sense; NOT the biological/evolutionary sense

nodes.label: Psychological Egoism
nodes.wd_description: The view that true altruism in humans is impossible

nodes.label: Hedonism
nodes.wd_description: philosophy of pleasure as the highest value

nodes.label: Eudaimonia
nodes.wd_description: Aristotelian term for happiness or welfare

nodes.label: Virtue
nodes.wd_description: morally positive trait or quality deemed to be morally good

nodes.label: Practical Wisdom
nodes.wd_description: ancient Greek word for a type of wisdom or intelligence

nodes.label: Golden Mean
nodes.note: Aristotelian doctrine; NOT the golden ratio

nodes.label: Akrasia
nodes.wd_description: lack of self-control or state of acting against one's better judgment

nodes.label: Practical Reason
nodes.wd_description: the use of reason to decide how to act

nodes.label: Moral Responsibility
nodes.wd_description: status of morally deserving praise, blame, reward, or punishment for an act or omission, in accordance with one's moral obligations

nodes.label: Free Will
nodes.wd_description: ability of agents to be the ultimate source or originator of their choices, free from external determination

nodes.label: Determinism
nodes.wd_description: philosophical belief that all events are determined completely by previously existing causes

nodes.label: Compatibilism
nodes.wd_description: belief that free will and determinism are mutually compatible to one another

nodes.label: Moral Dilemma
nodes.wd_description: decision-making problem between two conflicting moral imperatives, neither of which is unambiguously acceptable or preferable

nodes.label: Moral Status
nodes.wd_description: description of intrinsic self-worth and dignity
nodes.note: auto-resolution picked 'insects in ethics' — corrected by review 2026-06-11

nodes.label: Supererogation
nodes.wd_description: acts which are good but not morally required to be done

nodes.label: Doctrine of Double Effect
nodes.wd_description: set of ethical criteria permitting certain actions when one's otherwise legitimate act may also cause harm

nodes.label: Divine Command Theory
nodes.wd_description: theory that morality is commanded by, and originates only from, the divine

nodes.label: Natural Law
nodes.wd_description: system of law that is purportedly determined by nature, and is thus universal
nodes.note: ethical theory; NOT laws of nature in physics

nodes.label: Moral Sentimentalism
nodes.note: NOT literary sentimentalism

nodes.label: Is-Ought Problem
nodes.wd_description: philosophical problem articulated by David Hume in 1739 about how one can deduce prescriptive statements (what ought to be) from descriptive statements (what is)

nodes.label: Naturalistic Fallacy
nodes.wd_description: ethical argument asserting that it is fallacious to explain something good reductively

nodes.label: Open Question Argument
nodes.wd_description: philosophical argument put forward by British philosopher G. E. Moore, to refute the equating of the property of goodness with some non-moral property, X, whether naturalistic (e.g. pleasure) or supernatural

nodes.label: Prima Facie Duty
nodes.note: no Wikidata item (reviewed 2026-06-11)

nodes.label: Well-Being
nodes.wd_description: measure of how well life is to someone or a group with factors such as health, happiness and satisfaction

nodes.label: Justice
nodes.wd_description: broad idea of a situation where people receive that which they deserve

nodes.label: Rights
nodes.wd_description: fundamental legal, social, or ethical principles of freedom or entitlement according to some legal system, social convention, or ethical theory

nodes.label: Aristotle
nodes.wd_description: 4th-century BCE Classical Greek philosopher and polymath

nodes.label: Jeremy Bentham
nodes.wd_description: British philosopher, jurist, and social reformer (1748–1832)

nodes.label: Thomas Aquinas
nodes.wd_description: Italian Dominican friar, philosopher, Catholic priest, and Doctor of the Church (1225–1274)

nodes.label: Charles Stevenson
nodes.note: the emotivist philosopher; NOT the illustrator

nodes.label: Allan Gibbard
nodes.wd_description: American philosopher and social choice theorist

nodes.label: Stoicism
nodes.wd_description: school of Hellenistic philosophy who held that the practice of virtue suffices to attain eudaimonia

nodes.label: German Idealism
nodes.wd_description: predominant philosophical movement in Germany around 1800

nodes.label: Empiricism
nodes.wd_description: theory that states that knowledge comes only or primarily from sensory experience

nodes.label: Rationalism
nodes.wd_description: philosophical view that reason should be the chief source of knowledge
nodes.note: epistemological school (Descartes, Leibniz); NOT rationality generally

nodes.label: Scholasticism
nodes.wd_description: method of critical thought which dominated teaching by the academics ("scholastics", or "schoolmen") of medieval universities in Europe from about 1100 to 1700

nodes.label: Groundwork of the Metaphysics of Morals
nodes.wd_description: Translation of Kant's Groundwork published in 1997

nodes.label: The Metaphysics of Morals
nodes.wd_description: work of political and moral philosophy by Immanuel Kant
nodes.note: distinct from the 1785 Groundwork

nodes.label: An Enquiry Concerning the Principles of Morals
An Introduction to the Principles of Morals and Legislation
nodes.wd_description: intro to the Principles of Morals and Legislation

nodes.label: Utilitarianism
nodes.note: Mill's 1861 book; NOT the doctrine (see concept utilitarianism)

nodes.label: On Liberty
nodes.wd_description: non-fiction work by John Stuart Mill

nodes.label: The Right and the Good
nodes.wd_description: 1930 book by the Scottish philosopher David Ross

nodes.label: Language, Truth and Logic
nodes.wd_description: philosophical book by A. J. Ayer

nodes.label: A Theory of Justice
nodes.wd_description: work of political philosophy and ethics by John Rawls

nodes.label: Modern Moral Philosophy
nodes.note: Anscombe's 1958 journal article

nodes.label: Leviathan
aliases: Leviathan or The Matter, Forme and Power of a Commonwealth Ecclesiasticall and Civil

nodes.label: What We Owe to Each Other
slave_revolt_in_morality
nodes.note: auto-research around friedrich_nietzsche (nietzsche)

nodes.label: master_morality
nodes.note: auto-research around friedrich_nietzsche (nietzsche)
